name: Chalk Creek gravels
short_description: BLM-managed gravel beds along Chalk Creek where you can find quartz, feldspar, and garnet specimens. High-elevation Colorado rockhounding with no permit required.
description:
Chalk Creek gravels is a public BLM rockhounding site in the heart of Colorado's central mountain region, located near Salida in Chaffee County. This elevation 8672-foot site sits along the creek drainage where glacial activity and mountain weathering have concentrated mineral specimens in accessible gravel bars and stream beds.

The gravels here are primarily composed of quartz varieties, feldspar crystals, biotite mica, and small garnets weathered from the surrounding granite and metamorphic bedrock. The Chalk Creek drainage cuts through Precambrian granitic and gneissic formations that have been extensively fractured and weathered over millions of years, concentrating collectible minerals in the creek gravels and adjacent benches.

The gentle slope of this area makes it accessible for most rockhounders, though the high elevation means cooler temperatures and shorter collecting seasons. Visitors can work along gravel bars during low water periods and search the benches above the creek where older deposits are exposed. The combination of creek access and relatively flat terrain makes this a good site for families and those new to rockhounding in Colorado's mountains.

This is personal-use collecting only on BLM land with a 25-pound daily limit. The area sees moderate foot traffic during peak season, so arriving early in the day provides the best access to productive collecting areas. The high elevation and mountain setting mean weather can change quickly, so come prepared with layers and sun protection.
history: Chalk Creek gravels formed through glacial and periglacial weathering of Precambrian granite and metamorphic rocks during the Pleistocene ice ages. As mountain uplift exposed these mineral-rich formations and glaciers carved through the region, mineral-bearing gravels concentrated in the creek drainage, creating the collecting opportunities visible today along the streambed and terraces.
tips: Search the active gravel bars along Chalk Creek during late summer and early fall when water levels are lower, exposing more material. Use a small classifier or mesh screen to quickly process creek gravels and separate quartz and feldspar from finer sediment. Look for white, pink, and clear quartz specimens along with flesh-colored feldspar crystals embedded in the gravels; garnets typically appear as small dark red or brown rounded stones. Bring plenty of water at this elevation, as the dry air and altitude can cause dehydration quickly during collecting.
terrain_notes: Elevation 8672 feet with gentle slopes along Chalk Creek. Gravel bars and benches are accessible with minimal elevation gain from parking areas. Footing is uneven but manageable on loose gravels and rocky terrain. No wheelchair access. Walking distance to productive collecting areas typically 0.25 to 0.5 miles from parking. Sturdy hiking boots recommended due to loose material and creek crossings.
best_season: June - September. Higher elevation (8672 ft) keeps this site snow-covered in winter and spring runoff makes creek access difficult. Summer and early fall provide optimal conditions with lower water levels and stable weather, though afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August.
written_directions: From Salida, take US 285 north toward Buena Vista for approximately 10 miles. Look for signs toward Chalk Creek and Maysville. Turn west on County Road 162 (Chalk Creek Road) and follow it upstream for approximately 8 miles into the creek drainage. You will pass through private land initially, then enter BLM-managed land. Look for pullouts and parking areas along the creek road where you can safely park and access the gravels. A passenger vehicle can typically reach the lower collecting areas, but high-clearance vehicles are recommended for some of the upper drainage access points.
road_conditions: County Road 162 is a maintained dirt road with rough sections. High-clearance recommended but not required for lower drainage areas. Can be muddy or washed in spring runoff and after heavy rain. Some sections have washboard texture.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and wide shoulder areas along Chalk Creek Road where visitors park. No developed parking lot. Capacity varies by pullout, typically 2-6 vehicles per location. Best approach is to park along the road shoulder in wide areas near creek access points.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Hand tools and small hand equipment only. Do not damage streambanks or remove vegetation. Pack out all waste.
rules:

No motorized excavation

Pack out all waste
nearest_services: Salida, approximately 18 miles south. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively gentle terrain along creek gravels with uneven rocky footing. No wheelchair access. Loose gravel and rocks make walking slower than typical hiking. Bridges or creek crossings required at some collecting locations. Suitable for most hikers and families with kids age 6 and up. Sturdy footwear essential.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can I find at Chalk Creek gravels near Salida, Colorado?
faq.answer: Chalk Creek gravels are best known for quartz specimens (clear, white, and pink varieties), feldspar crystals, biotite mica flakes, and small garnets. These minerals are weathered from the surrounding Precambrian granite and metamorphic bedrock into the creek gravels where they accumulate and are accessible for collection. The quartz is often found in clusters or points, while feldspar shows characteristic flesh-colored or pink crystals.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Chalk Creek gravels in Colorado?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Chalk Creek gravels. This is BLM public land where personal-use collecting is allowed under standard FLPMA regulations. You must follow the 25-pound daily collection limit and use hand tools only. No commercial collection is permitted.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Chalk Creek gravels for rockhounding?
faq.answer: June through September is the optimal collecting season. At 8672 feet elevation, this site is snow-covered or muddy during winter and spring. Summer months provide lower creek water levels that expose more gravel bars, though afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August. Early fall (September) typically offers the most stable weather and best water conditions.

faq.question: Is Chalk Creek gravels suitable for beginner rockhounders and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Chalk Creek gravels is beginner-friendly and family-suitable. The gentle terrain along creek gravels requires no technical hiking skills, and the quartz and feldspar specimens are easy to identify. Children ages 6 and up can participate. The main challenges are the high elevation (which can cause altitude effects) and loose, uneven footing. Bring plenty of water and sturdy shoes.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to collect at Chalk Creek gravels?
faq.answer: Basic hand tools work best here: a small classifier or mesh screen for processing creek gravels, a hand shovel or trowel, and a 5-gallon bucket. The 25-pound daily limit means you won't need heavy equipment. Wear sturdy hiking boots for loose gravel footing, bring sun protection and plenty of water for the high elevation, and include a headlamp if you plan to collect into evening hours.

faq.question: How do I reach Chalk Creek gravels from Salida and what are road conditions like?
faq.answer: From Salida, take US 285 north for 10 miles, then turn west on County Road 162 (Chalk Creek Road) and follow it upstream for 8 miles to BLM land. The road is a maintained dirt road that is passable with a passenger vehicle, though high-clearance is recommended for upper drainage areas. The road can be rough or muddy after heavy rain or spring runoff. Multiple informal pullouts allow parking along the creek road where you can access gravels on foot.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and garnet at Chalk Creek gravels near Salida, Colorado. BLM public land, no permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Year-round access.
